Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study in bursts when I felt like it or when something was due soon. Most of my studying happened late at night when my roommates were asleep and I could actually focus. I know I should've been more organized, but with everything going on, I just couldn't stick to a schedule. Some weeks I'd put in a lot of hours, other weeks barely anything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Deadlines were pretty much the only thing that got me to sit down and work consistently. I'd have these stretches where I wouldn't touch anything for days, then suddenly I'd be cramming for hours because something was due. My work schedule and financial stress made it impossible to have regular study times.
Q3: Usually it was because I needed to check if there was something due or to download materials. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a deadline and that would push me to log in. I wasn't someone who browsed the LMS just to see what was new. It was more like a tool I used when I absolutely had to, not something I engaged with regularly.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a habit for me. There were maybe a couple weeks where I tried to check in daily, but that fell apart quickly. Life got in the way, and I found other ways to study that didn't require me to be online constantly. The peer support group helped remind me about stuff sometimes, which was the closest thing to routine I had.
Q5: The reading materials and lecture notes were the most useful to me since I learn best by reading and writing things out. I'd download PDFs and work through them on my own time. The discussion posts were occasionally helpful when other students explained concepts differently than the instructor. But honestly, I got most value from materials I could take offline.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the videos most of the time. Watching them felt like it took forever and I couldn't skim through like I can with text. I also didn't engage much with optional readings or extra resources. When you're stressed about money and time, you start cutting corners on anything that doesn't directly affect your grade.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection between an activity and doing well on an exam or assignment, it felt worth it. Anything that seemed like busywork or padding, I'd skip. I also valued things I could do on my own schedule without needing to be online at specific times. Time is something I don't have a lot of, so efficiency mattered.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. I know that sounds bad, but when you're juggling everything I am, you have to prioritize. Required activities got done because they had to. Optional ones? I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. There's always something more pressing when you're barely keeping your head above water.
Q9: Absolutely. Most of my actual studying happened offline. I'd download everything at once when I had internet access, then study from printed notes or saved files. I have a notebook full of handwritten summaries that I made from the course materials. I'd read and rewrite concepts because that's how things stick for me. None of that shows up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were whole weeks where I was actively studying but never logged in. I'd work from my printed materials at the library or at home. Sometimes I'd study with a couple of classmates in person, going over concepts together. The LMS would show nothing, but I was actually putting in serious work during those times. My exam scores reflect that, even if my login history doesn't.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in once, download everything available for that module, and then not come back until the next set of materials dropped. My internet at home isn't always reliable, so it made more sense to grab everything and study offline. I'd spend hours with those downloaded files, but the LMS would only show that one brief login.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when everything piled up. I was dealing with some financial stuff that had me really stressed and sad. It was hard to care about coursework when I was worried about more basic things. The peer support helped pull me out of it somewhat, but there were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage at all.
Q13: Constantly. I work part-time and that eats into my study time significantly. When I'm exhausted from work, the last thing I want to do is log into the LMS and watch videos or post in discussions. Confusion about assignments sometimes made me avoid them entirely until the last minute. It's easier to procrastinate when you're not sure what you're supposed to be doing.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd be logged in but completely zoned out. I'd have the page open but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about bills or just feeling down. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. Those sessions were basically useless even though they probably showed up as engagement time.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they mattered. Part of it was anxiety, I think. The bigger something seemed, the more I'd avoid starting it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuse. It's a bad habit I've had for years, and stress makes it worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly kick into gear. Most of my LMS activity happened in the 24-48 hours before something was due. It's not healthy, but it's how I've survived this long academically.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually got it in time to use it. If I got comments on an assignment, I'd try to apply them to the next one. But sometimes feedback came too late or was too vague to be useful. Positive feedback definitely motivated me to keep going during rough patches. Knowing someone noticed my effort meant something.
Q18: The structure was okay, but it assumed students had more time and consistent access than I do. Weekly modules with multiple components felt overwhelming sometimes. I appreciated when things were clearly laid out, but the sheer volume of content made it hard to keep up. A more flexible structure would have helped someone in my situation.
Q19: Having downloadable materials was huge for me, that made engagement possible. But the emphasis on discussion posts and video content made things harder. I'm not great at participating in online discussions, and videos don't match how I learn. More reading-based options and flexible deadlines would have made a real difference for me.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for answers or trying to figure out what's expected without actually doing the work. Or maybe they're like me sometimes, logged in but mentally checked out. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. You can click around a lot without actually learning or producing anything meaningful.
Q21: That's basically my story. My attendance and LMS activity look terrible, but I still pulled a Distinction because I studied hard offline. I know the material, I just don't engage with it through the platform. Some of us learn differently and don't need to be constantly logged in to succeed. The grades should speak for themselves.
Q22: Logs can show when you accessed things and for how long, but they can't show if you actually understood anything. They miss all the offline work, the thinking, the handwritten notes, the conversations with classmates. They're measuring presence, not learning. A student could have perfect logs and learn nothing, or terrible logs and ace the course.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when you're genuinely curious and putting mental effort into learning, whether that's online or offline. For me, engagement happened in my notebook and during late-night study sessions, not in my login frequency. It's internal, not something a system can easily measure.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I struggled emotionally this semester, or how hard I worked despite feeling sad and stressed most of the time. It wouldn't show the peer support conversations that kept me going, or the hours I spent with printed materials. It definitely wouldn't show that despite looking disengaged, I actually cared deeply about doing well. The numbers tell one story, but my reality is completely different.
